data.title: Staidson BioPharma Obtains Drug Manufacturing License to Produce STSP-0601 Injection; Shares Jump 12%
data.description:
Staidson Beijing BioPharmaceuticals secured a revised drug manufacturing license from a Beijing regulator to produce STSP-0601 for injection by Jiangsu Beijietai Biotechnology.

The license is valid until Dec. 7, according to a Thursday filing with the Shenzhen bourse.

Formal production may start only after the marketing authorization holder obtains drug approval documents and passes Good Manufacturing Practice compliance inspections.

Shares of Staidson BioPharma jumped 12% in recent trade.

data.title: Solis Minerals Starts Drilling at Peru Project; Shares Fall 9%
data.description:
Solis Minerals has begun a 2,500-meter diamond drilling program at its Chancho al Palo copper project in Peru, according to a Friday filing with the Australian bourse.

Assay results are expected by July, the filing said.

Shares of the company fell 9% at market close.

data.title: EssilorLuxottica to Buy European Eye Hospitals Operator Optegra
data.description:
EssilorLuxottica said Friday it agreed to buy the integrated ophthalmology platform Optegra from European private equity investor MidEuropa.

The European eyewear company said the acquisition supports its medical technology strategy. Completion is expected later in 2025, subject to customary closing conditions, including regulatory approvals.

data.title: U.S. Treasury Yields Barely Move, Await PCE Data — Market Talk
data.description:
0623 GMT - U.S. Treasury yields are little changed in early European trading after Thursday's declines across maturities as markets await PCE inflation data--the Federal Reserve's preferred gauge of inflation--at 1230 GMT. However, some market watchers still see room for short-term gains for Treasurys. "My overall biases are still towards some short-term Treasury gains as we cruise towards next Friday's NFP [nonfarm payroll] print," says Pepperstone's Michael Brown in a note. The two-year Treasury yield rises 1 basis point to 3.947%; the 10-year yield is up 0.4 basis point at 4.428%, while the 30-year is flat at 4.920%, according to Tradeweb. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0603 GMT - Nestle is facing rising currency headwinds, with recent strength in the Swiss franc relative to the dollar likely to offset the company's organic sales growth, Berenberg analysts say in a research note. "Since the start of 2025, the Swiss franc has strengthened by 10% versus the U.S. dollar, and our model suggests the currency translation effect to Nestle's group sales in 2025 will be [minus] 4.2%, more than offsetting organic sales growth of 3.4%," the analysts say. Currency headwinds are set to hurt the Swiss food company's profitability as well, they add. Berenberg cuts its target price on Nestle to 87 francs from 90 francs. Shares closed 1.35% lower at 87.51 francs on Thursday and are up nearly 17% since the start of 2025. (adria.calatayud@wsj.com)

0558 GMT - Tariff-induced market volatility has decreased somewhat, but longer-maturity U.S. Treasury yields remain well above their April 2 tariff announcement-day levels, TD Securities' Jan Nevruzi and Gennadiy Goldberg say in a note. The long-end-driven steepening of risk-free curves has been a global phenomenon, with longer-dated bond yields moving higher in the U.S., U.K., Europe, Japan and other major bond markets, the U.S. rates strategists say. U.S. Treasurys have been underperforming, they say. That said, the U.S. Treasury could hint at long-end auction size decreases as soon as the August refunding, the Senate could push back on the reconciliation bill's fiscal impact, and softening data could lead investors to pencil in a more dovish Fed, they say. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0543 GMT - Bond issuance pressure in the eurozone is set to moderate meaningfully in 2H relative to 1H, and at the margin this should support European duration, Morgan Stanley Research's rates strategists say in a note. They forecast gross issuance in 2H to decrease by about 35% versus 1H, with issuance net of redemptions and of the European Central Bank's quantitative tightening halving versus 1H. The strategists forecast gross government bond issuance of 495 billion euros in 2H, down from 772 billion euros expected in 1H. "This should help provide some relief to European yields in 2H25," they say. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0543 GMT - U.S. Treasurys have been a busy economic barometer as of late and the net effect of all the tumult is that the average yield on the U.S. Treasury index is about 20 basis points higher than before President Trump's tariff announcements on April 2, says Federated Hermes' R.J. Gallo in a note. U.S. budget deliberations are not yet finished and the Senate could end up cutting more spending from the bill, the senior portfolio manager for fixed income says. "The bond market would welcome any additional fiscal restraint added to the bill," he says. "Conversely, should Senate moderate push in the opposite direction to water down Medicaid cuts, the opposite net effect could occur, and Treasury yields may rise somewhat." (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0508 GMT - A trade court ruling on Trump's "Liberation Day" tariffs could limit the U.S. push to strike trade deals, Citi analysts say. While under appeal, markets may redraw some of their original assumptions, they write. Treasury Secretary Bessent told Fox News that the ruling hasn't had an impact on negotiations. "We have not seen any of that in terms of our trading partners, they are coming to us in good faith and trying to complete the deals before the 90-day pause ends," he said. "We've seen no change in their attitude in the past 48 hours." He characterized U.S.-China talks as "a bit stalled," adding that a Trump-Xi call is likely needed given their magnitude and complexity. Bessent said the two leaders have a "very good relationship" and expressed confidence that China will come to the table. (fabiana.negrinochoa@wsj.com)

0339 GMT - The Singapore dollar weakens slightly against its U.S. counterpart amid mild risk-off sentiment spurred by losses across most regional equity markets. The U.S. tariffs on Asia will probably remain amid the legal battle over President Trump's tariffs, MUFG Bank's Michael Wan says in a research report. These "tariffs are likely to hurt Asia much more than others given our region's export-oriented nature as a broad generalization," the senior currency analyst says. Hence, MUFG Bank continues to expect Asian currencies' underperformance against G-10 currencies. USD/SGD is 0.1% higher at 1.2883. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0328 GMT - The Malaysian ringgit may trade within the 4.20-4.25 range against the U.S. dollar next week, says Kenanga IB economists in a note. Weak U.S. data and concerns over U.S. fiscal health persist, and could pressure the dollar, they say. Any pro-market surprises from Trump, especially on tariffs, could lift the dollar index toward 100, they reckon. Despite recent developments, markets are unlikely to fully unwind the dollar risk premium, they note. Technically, the pair's outlook appears neutral and could hover around 4.23, they add. Kenanga pegs USD/MYR's short-term resistance at 4.24 and support at 4.21. USD/MYR is 0.2% lower at 4.2350. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

0305 GMT - Uncertainty over the trade tensions remains high, despite the U.S. court's decision to invalidate Trump's tariffs, according to Nomura analysts in a research note. The Trump administration appears to have already filed an appeal to this court order, and the administration could also use other ways to implement those invalidated tariffs, the analysts say. "We think these developments will also complicate the picture on trade deals," the analysts say, adding that this may slow the process of trade negotiations. Asian equity markets will remain choppy driven by newsflow related to U.S. trade policies, they say.(tracy.qu@wsj.com)

0303 GMT - Recent volatility in JGB market is "bound to unnerve investors," three members of HSBC Global Research say in a research report. "Insurers, the traditional and stable domestic investor base of super-long JGBs, have been sitting on the sidelines," they say. A volatile environment keeps "price-sensitive private sector participants more cautious, likely awaiting more clarity on a number of variables before wading back into the market," the members say, citing variables including monetary policy. There is also worry over "how the volatility in the long-end could reverberate through to the shorter part of the curve, which will then have an impact on the real economy," they add. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0243 GMT - Strong inflationary pressures in Tokyo lift BOJ rate-hike odds, ING's Min Joo Kang says in a research report. Core consumer prices excluding fresh food in Tokyo rose 3.6% in May versus the 3.4% increase in April, and beat market consensus for a 3.5% rise, the senior economist for South Korea and Japan notes. Also, core-core consumer prices excluding fresh food and energy in Tokyo rose 3.3% in May versus 3.1% growth in April, the economist notes. Although Japanese economic data released Friday paint a mixed picture of the economy, the Bank of Japan is likely to prioritize price pressures, the economist says. A 25bp rate increase by the BOJ is expected in July, the economist adds. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0202 GMT - South Korea's headline consumer inflation likely eased slightly in May, justifying the central bank's resumption of rate cuts to support the economy. The median forecast from a WSJ poll of seven analysts is for a 2.0% on-year rise in the benchmark consumer-price index, following a 2.1% gain in April. The index is tipped to have remained flat on month in May after edging up 0.1% in the previous month. The Bank of Korea ended a pause in its easing cycle on Thursday, cutting its policy rate by a quarter percentage point to 2.50%. The inflation data are due on Wednesday. (kwanwoo.jun@wsj.com)

data.title: Ind-Swift Gets Renewed Approval from UK Drug Regulator for Punjab, India Unit
data.description:
Ind-Swift has received renewed approval from the Medicines & Healthcare products Regulatory Agency (MHRA) for its export-oriented unit in Punjab, India, for three years, according to a Thursday filing with the Indian stock exchanges.

The approval is based on quality metrics and quality systems.

The company's shares were up 3% in recent trade.

data.title: Muthoot Finance to reissue April 2030 bonds, bankers say
data.description:
India's Muthoot Finance plans to raise 15 billion rupees ($175.5 million), including a greenshoe option of 10 billion rupees, through the reissue of 8.20% April 2030 bonds, three bankers said on Friday.

The non-banking finance company has invited coupon and commitment bids from bankers and investors on Monday, they said.

Muthoot Finance did not reply to a Reuters email seeking comment.
